claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your situation.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. I'm asking about my son Alex, who is 17 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I'd like to learn more about Alex's situation.

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (For example, is he in school, college, sixth form, or doing an approved training course?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. He's finished school but he's not doing anything like college or sixth form right now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. I have a few more questions about Alex's situation.

**Has Alex registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services?** And if so, did he leave education or training within the last 20 weeks?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't registered with any careers service or anything like that. He just left school and hasn't signed up for any of those services.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have a couple more questions to complete the assessment:

**Is Alex currently working, and if so, how many hours per week does he work?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't working at the moment. He's not doing any job right now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just a couple final questions:

**Is Alex claiming any benefits in his own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not claiming any benefits in his own name. He doesn't receive anything like that himself.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. One last question to complete the assessment:

**Do you live in the UK with Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK and Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, **I'm sorry to inform you that you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex** at this time.

Here's why:

Since Alex is 17 years old, he falls into the "16 or over" category. For children aged 16-19, you can continue to receive Child Benefit **only if** they are in approved education or training. 

Currently, Alex is:
- Not in any approved education or training (such as school, college, or sixth form)
- Not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services (which would allow you to claim for an additional 20 weeks after leaving education)
- Not working

**However, this situation could change.** If Alex decides to:
- Return to approved education or training, or
- Register with a careers service (if he only recently left school within the last 20 weeks)

You may then become eligible for Child Benefit. If his circumstances change, you would need to make a new claim or contact HMRC to update them.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old, which means he falls into the '16 or over' category for Child Benefit. According to the rules, you normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 AND they stay in approved education or training. However, Alex is not currently in any approved education or training. Additionally, while you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, Alex has not registered with either of these services. Since Alex is not in approved education or training and has not registered with a careers service or armed services, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with their 17-year-old son Alex. While the claimant would normally qualify for Child Benefit for a child over 16, Alex must be in approved education or training (which he is not), or have recently left education and registered with a careers service or armed services to extend eligibility for 20 weeks (which he has not done). As Alex meets neither of these criteria, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for him.

Since Alex is not in approved education or training and has not registered with a careers service or armed services, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

While the claimant would normally qualify for Child Benefit for a child over 16, Alex must be in approved education or training (which he is not), or have recently left education and registered with a careers service or armed services to extend eligibility for 20 weeks (which he has not done). As Alex meets neither of these criteria, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for him.
